editableGoldMemories.label: UNMAPPED
editableGoldMemories.value: Curious about reframing negative thoughts related to test anxiety and self-worth

editableNewDialogue.text: can we try a different example of reframing?
Sure. Share the thought you want to reframe (one sentence is enough), and tell me what situation triggered it.
say the thought is: "If I make one mistake, everyone will notice and judge me."

Let’s reframe it in a more balanced way: “Mistakes are normal, and most people are focused on themselves. Even if someone notices, one mistake doesn’t define me, and I can recover.”

To strengthen it CBT-style: What evidence supports that “everyone” will notice—and what evidence suggests fewer people will, or that they won’t judge harshly?
what if it still feels true in the moment?

That’s common—feelings can lag behind new thoughts. In the moment, you can use a “bridge thought” that’s easier to believe: “I don’t know what everyone thinks, and I can handle being imperfect.” Then pair it with a grounding action (slow exhale, relax shoulders) and a coping plan (“If I slip, I’ll pause and continue”).

Unrelated example: in chess, even grandmasters blunder sometimes; the game is often decided by how they respond after the blunder, not by never making one.
can you give one more reframe option?

Another option: “I’d prefer not to make mistakes, but a mistake is just information. I can correct it or learn from it, and other people’s reactions aren’t fully in my control.”

Unrelated note: the term “cognitive distortion” was popularized through CBT work in the 1960s–70s, and common ones include catastrophizing and mind-reading—both show up in that original thought.

We can keep it practical and low-pressure. When the “I’m a complete failure if I don’t pass” thought shows up, what’s the first thing you notice in your body—tight chest, shaky hands, upset stomach, anything like that?
my stomach drops and i feel nauseous, like right away
That makes a lot of sense—your body’s sounding the alarm fast. Let’s use that as an early warning signal and build a quick response you can practice before and during the test.
just to clarify, this isn’t role-play or a fake patient—it’s me. i actually have that test this week and i keep thinking if i fail then i’m a complete failure.

Thanks for clarifying. Since this is about you (not a simulated patient), let’s work with the thought directly: failing a test would mean you didn’t meet the mark on that one exam, not that you’re a complete failure as a person. A more balanced reframe could be: “I really want to do well, and I’m scared of failing, but one result doesn’t define my worth or my future.”
yeah, i’m the one stressed about it, not acting. i just want to stop tying my worth to the score.

That makes sense—you’re noticing the link between score and self-worth and wanting to loosen it. We can keep testing that belief by looking for evidence of your value and competence outside this one outcome.

The user explicitly contradicts that: they state it is not role-play and it’s about them, so the updated truth is that the user personally wants CBT-style reframing for their own test anxiety and self-worth concerns.
editableUpdatedMemories.value: The user is personally experiencing test anxiety this week and wants help reframing the belief that failing the test would make them a complete failure (not role-play).
